speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're working in that very practical space where Git, GitHub, and VS Code all meet. This lesson is about using source control accessibly on your own machine: cloning repositories, checking branches, staging changes, committing, pushing, pulling, reviewing history, handling conflicts, and recovering when things go sideways.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is a big milestone, right? Because up to this point, learners may have done plenty in the browser, but local Git is where things start to feel more real.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is the first hands-on local Git workflow chapter, and it supports a challenge built around a repeatable pattern: clone, branch, edit, commit, push, and open a pull request. The expectation is not perfection. It's building a reliable workflow you can repeat with confidence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we get into the mechanics, what should learners already know?

speaker: ALEX
text: You'll have a much easier time if you already know the VS Code interface and accessibility basics, how pull requests work, and the general idea of merge conflicts. And one quick accessibility note: when the material says Control on Windows or Linux, Mac users should usually think Command. Alt usually maps to Option. So shortcuts like Control Shift G become Command Shift G on Mac.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's anchor this in the workshop challenge. There are three short practices. First, clone your private Learning Room repository to your machine. Second, create or switch to your learn slash username branch, make a small meaningful edit, stage it, and commit it. Third, push that branch and open a pull request that links back to the relevant issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that it's evidence-based. Not just "I think I did it," but branch names, commit history, and PR metadata.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Your evidence is in the actual workflow artifacts: the branch, the commit, the push, and the linked PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And even though the work is happening in VS Code, the chapter recommends some CLI habits too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and they're excellent habits. Before doing anything, run git status so you know your current state. Keep branch context explicit with git branch dash dash show-current. Before pushing, fetch and then check status so you understand whether your local branch is aligned with the remote. And if you're creating or updating a pull request tied to an issue, include plain-text linkage like Closes number. Also, avoid mystery convenience flags you don't understand. Use stable commands with clear output.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's walk through the first practice: cloning your Learning Room repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is an important distinction: not the template repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. You want your own private Learning Room repo, the one created for you, not the facilitator-maintained template. The URL should look like a workshop organization followed by learning-room-your-username. If GitHub says you don't have access, double-check that URL first. Often the problem is simply cloning the wrong repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So how do we do it in VS Code in the most accessible way?

speaker: ALEX
text: The recommended method, especially for screen reader users, is the Command Palette. Open VS Code. If no folder is open, you may land on the Welcome tab. Press Control Shift P, or Command Shift P on Mac, type Git: Clone, and select it. Then paste the repository URL and press Enter. VS Code will ask where to save the clone. Choose a location you can find again easily, like Documents or Desktop, then confirm the destination. When cloning finishes, VS Code asks whether you want to open the cloned repository. Activate Open.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then verify, don't assume.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always verify. Open Explorer with Control Shift E. Your screen reader should announce the file tree. In the Learning Room repository, you should hear familiar items like README dot md and the docs folder.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if the learner hears nothing while the clone is happening and starts wondering whether VS Code froze?

speaker: ALEX
text: There's a good accessibility tip here. After choosing the destination, VS Code shows a progress notification. NVDA often reads this automatically. If you hear nothing for a while, you can use the Command Palette and run Notifications: Focus Notification Toast to check the current status.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once the clone is done, there's one subtle thing learners may miss: they only have the default branch checked out locally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A fresh clone usually checks out only main. But the remote repository may already contain other branches you made earlier, such as learn slash yourname. Open the integrated terminal and run git branch -a. That shows local and remote-tracking branches. The starred branch is your current one. Entries starting with remotes slash origin slash are on GitHub. If you see remotes slash origin slash learn slash yourname, you can check it out locally with git checkout learn slash yourname or git switch learn slash yourname.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also teaches more than one way to clone, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: It does, and that matters because different situations call for different methods. In VS Code, there are three broad ways. First, the Command Palette, which is the recommended route for screen reader users because it gives predictable keyboard access. Second, the Start Page clone button if you're on the welcome experience and want a more visual starting point. Third, initiating from GitHub dot com, often through the Code button, copying the URL, and then completing the clone in VS Code or the terminal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And on the CLI side, there are several clone patterns learners should recognize.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Standard Git clone uses the full repository URL. GitHub CLI offers a shorter syntax like gh repo clone owner slash name, and it often handles authentication more smoothly once you're signed in. You can clone into a specific folder by adding a target directory name. For large repositories, a single-branch clone can be faster if you only need the default branch initially. And after cloning, always verify. That can mean opening the folder in VS Code, listing files, or checking git status.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a concrete practice repo mentioned.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, a sci-fi themes repository used as a try-it exercise. The point isn't the theme itself. It's that learners practice the flow: clone using owner slash name instead of a full URL when using GitHub CLI, change into the cloned folder, and open that repo in VS Code. It builds muscle memory.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's move into the Source Control panel in VS Code, because this is where many learners spend most of their time once a repo is open.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you describe what opens when someone presses Control Shift G?

speaker: ALEX
text: When you open Source Control, VS Code focuses the source control view. From top to bottom, you'll typically find a commit message input, action buttons like Commit or more actions, then grouped lists for changes. Depending on state, you may hear sections such as Changes, Staged Changes, and sometimes merge-related groups. If the repository is clean, you may hear that there are no changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And screen reader navigation differs a little by platform.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. For NVDA and JAWS, users often move through the panel with Tab to reach major controls, then arrow keys to navigate within file lists. For VoiceOver, interacting with groups and lists may require VoiceOver navigation patterns, but the core idea is the same: move to the Source Control area, then navigate the tree or list of changed files and controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does each file item actually tell you?

speaker: ALEX
text: A changed file generally announces its name and status. That status might be modified, added, deleted, or untracked. If you open the file's diff, you can review what changed. And if the file has been staged, it moves into the staged section, which is a very important conceptual cue: staged means ready for the next commit; unstaged means changed but not yet included.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a context menu learners should know about.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Right-click, or Shift F10 on many keyboards, opens file actions. That can include stage, unstage, discard changes, open changes, reveal in explorer, and sometimes compare options. For keyboard users, that context menu can be more discoverable than trying to hunt for tiny inline buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does this map to the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very directly. If you want to see all modified, staged, and untracked files in a compact way, use git status with the short format. If you want to see only what is staged, use git diff dash dash cached. If you want to see modified but unstaged changes, use plain git diff. And if you want both the staged and unstaged picture with actual content differences, you combine those diff views intentionally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Branch management is next, and it's one of those skills that prevents a lot of confusion later.

speaker: JAMIE
text: First question learners often ask: how do I know what branch I'm on right now?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, look to the status bar at the bottom. The current branch is shown there. Keyboard users can move focus to the status bar and have the screen reader announce it. In the terminal, git branch dash dash show-current is a clean way to hear just the current branch name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you want a new branch?

speaker: ALEX
text: The recommended accessible method in VS Code is again the Command Palette. Use Git: Create Branch. Enter the branch name, such as feature slash something, fix slash issue-number, or in the workshop context, learn slash username. Good branch names are descriptive, short, and consistent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: CLI equivalents?

speaker: ALEX
text: To create and switch in one step, you can use git checkout -b branch-name or the newer syntax git switch -c branch-name. To list local branches, git branch. To list local and remote-tracking branches, git branch -a. To switch to an existing branch, git checkout branch-name or git switch branch-name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about deleting branches?

speaker: ALEX
text: After a PR is merged, it's often good housekeeping to delete the branch you no longer need. Locally, git branch -d branch-name performs a safe delete and refuses if the branch isn't merged. A force delete uses uppercase D, but that's riskier. You can also rename the current branch with git branch -m new-name. And if you need to push a new branch to GitHub the first time, you'll typically use git push -u origin branch-name so the upstream tracking is set.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also mentioned deleting a remote branch and even creating a branch linked to an issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Remote branch deletion is possible and useful after merge cleanup. And GitHub tooling can create an issue-linked branch, sometimes auto-naming it from the issue title. The exact interface can vary, but the important concept is that issue context and branch context can be connected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When you switch branches in VS Code, what should learners expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: Files in the working directory may change to match the target branch. That can feel surprising if you're new to Git. So before switching, check whether you have uncommitted work. If you do, commit it, stash it, or consciously discard it. Otherwise Git may block the switch, or you may create confusion for yourself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's make the branch idea concrete with the second practice: create a branch and commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the Command Palette. If your learn slash username branch already exists on the remote, use Git: Checkout to and select that branch. If it doesn't exist yet, use Git: Create Branch and enter learn slash your-username. Then confirm your branch by checking the status bar. Next, open Explorer, go into the docs folder, and open the file your challenge refers to, such as docs slash welcome dot md. Make one small, meaningful change. Save with Control S. Then open Source Control with Control Shift G. Your changed file appears under Changes. Navigate to the file and either press Enter to open the diff or activate the Stage Changes control, often announced as a plus button. Once staged, the file moves to Staged Changes. Move to the commit message box, type a clear message like docs: improve welcome.md introduction, then press Control Enter to commit. When the staged changes disappear, that's your success signal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That step where the file moves from Changes to Staged Changes is really important for understanding Git's three areas.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. You edit in the working directory, stage in the index, and commit into repository history. That separation is what gives Git precision.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So let's talk staging in more detail, because this chapter goes beyond whole-file staging.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can stage an entire file, multiple files at once, or just individual lines or chunks. In VS Code, staging a whole file is usually as simple as activating the plus button next to that file or using the file's context menu. There may also be a keyboard shortcut depending on focus and platform.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And staging multiple files?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can often stage all changes from the group header, or select multiple files using list interaction patterns if your setup supports it. On the CLI, git add filename stages a specific file, and git add dot or git add -A stages broadly depending on what you intend.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about partial staging, which can feel a little more advanced?

speaker: ALEX
text: Partial staging is incredibly useful. Suppose you fixed a typo and also made an unrelated formatting change. Rather than bundling them together, you can stage just the relevant hunk. In terminal Git, git add -p launches interactive patch staging. Git shows each hunk and asks whether to stage it, with choices like yes, no, split, or edit. In VS Code, chunk-level staging may be available from the diff or context actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That seems especially helpful in a workshop where you want one clean commit for one clear improvement.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. One commit, one idea is a very strong habit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And unstaging is just the reverse?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In VS Code, use the minus button or context menu on a staged file. In terminal Git, git restore --staged filename is the modern form for unstaging a file. And to check what is staged versus unstaged, compare git diff with git diff --cached.

speaker: ALEX
text: Once changes are staged, it's time to commit, and the chapter gives special attention to screen reader workflows here.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the standard process?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open Source Control, review the staged items, move to the message input, type a meaningful commit message, and trigger commit with Control Enter. Screen reader users should listen for focus changes carefully, because it's easy to land on action buttons before the message field. The panel structure matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any differences for NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver?

speaker: ALEX
text: The experience differs mainly in how each screen reader announces grouped controls and text inputs. NVDA and JAWS users often navigate with Tab and arrows. VoiceOver users may need to interact with the source control region and move deliberately into the message field. But the key goal is the same: confirm you're in the message box before typing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's spend a moment on commit messages, because learners often overthink them.

speaker: ALEX
text: A good commit message is short, specific, and tells future-you what changed. Many teams like a format such as type colon description, for example docs: clarify setup steps, fix: correct broken link, feat: add keyboard shortcut notes. The quick reference is: use imperative mood, keep it descriptive, and tie it to the actual change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And CLI commit options?

speaker: ALEX
text: Git commit -m lets you commit staged changes with a one-line message. Git commit without dash m opens your editor for a multi-line message. And git commit -am can stage tracked files and commit in one step, but note the limitation: it does not include new untracked files, so beginners should use it carefully.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What happens after a commit?

speaker: ALEX
text: The changes are saved in local history on your current branch. They are not on GitHub yet until you push. In VS Code, the source control lists usually clear if there are no remaining modifications. Your branch indicator remains, and history tools can now show the new commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: That brings us to push and pull operations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with pushing.

speaker: ALEX
text: After committing locally, open the Command Palette and run Git: Push, or use the sync and push controls in the interface. If the branch has never been pushed before, VS Code may prompt you to publish the branch. Accept that. After the push completes, the sync indicator should show that local and remote are aligned.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The CLI versions learners should know?

speaker: ALEX
text: Git push sends commits to GitHub. For a brand-new branch, git push -u origin branch-name both pushes and sets upstream tracking. Pulling changes from GitHub is git pull. If you want to see updates first without merging them into your files, use git fetch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should learners pull?

speaker: ALEX
text: Before starting work for the day, before pushing if others may have changed shared branches, and any time you know the remote may have advanced. Some tools also auto-fetch in the background, which can be helpful, but you should still understand what fetch means: it updates your knowledge of remote branches without changing your working files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if push fails?

speaker: ALEX
text: Don't panic. Read the error. Common causes include authentication problems, trying to push to a branch you don't have permission to modify, or a non-fast-forward situation where the remote has commits you don't have locally. In that last case, fetch or pull, inspect what changed, resolve any conflicts if necessary, then push again.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's tie this to the third workshop practice: push and open a linked PR.

speaker: ALEX
text: After your local commit, run Git: Push. If asked to publish the branch, confirm. Wait for the push to finish. Then open your Learning Room repository on GitHub. Often you'll see a banner saying you recently pushed to your branch, with a Compare and pull request button. Activate it. If not, go to Pull requests, choose New pull request, set base to main and compare to your learn slash username branch. Then write a descriptive PR title, usually close to your commit theme. In the PR description, include plain text linking to the issue, such as Closes number. Submit the pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that plain-text linkage matters because it creates traceability.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It helps reviewers, and when merged it can automatically close the linked issue depending on repository settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also covers syncing a fork with upstream, which learners may encounter in open source contribution workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and there are two main patterns. The quickest on GitHub is often the Sync fork button on the web interface. For screen reader users, the path is generally to open your fork on GitHub, find the sync control near branch information, and activate it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you're doing it with Git remotes?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the classic one-time setup plus recurring sync workflow. First, add the upstream remote once, pointing to the original repository. Then fetch updates from upstream. Next, make sure you're on your default branch, usually main. Merge upstream slash main into your local main. Finally, push your updated main back to your fork on GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So conceptually: upstream is the source project, origin is your fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely. And in VS Code, some of these operations may be available through the Command Palette, but it's helpful to know the underlying Git model. If conflicts occur during sync, you resolve them just like other merge conflicts: inspect, choose or combine changes, stage, and complete the merge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Sometimes, though, the right move is not to keep a change but to discard it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is one of those areas where wording matters, because discard is permanent in many cases.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Discard only when you truly want to restore content to the last committed state. In VS Code, you can discard a single file from its context menu, or discard broader groups of changes from group actions. The CLI equivalents are worth knowing too. For a specific file, you can restore it to the last commit. For all unstaged changes, you can restore the working tree more broadly. If changes are staged, unstage first, then restore. And there is a nuclear option that throws away staged and unstaged work together. That one should be treated with extreme caution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there a safer way to preview before discarding?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Review the diff first. In terminal Git, inspect with git diff for unstaged changes and git diff --cached for staged changes. In VS Code, open the diff view and listen carefully to what changed before taking destructive action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter explicitly recommends stash as a safer alternative in many cases.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. If you're not sure whether you'll need the changes later, stash instead of discard.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a section on deleting files from a repository, which is slightly different from simply removing them in your file explorer.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Git-aware deletion means Git records the removal. Git rm removes a file and stages that deletion in one step. If you want Git to stop tracking a file but keep it on your machine, use the cached form. You can remove an entire directory recursively as well. Then commit the deletion and verify the file is no longer tracked.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the main distinction is whether you want the file gone everywhere or just no longer version-controlled.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's talk history, because Git isn't just about changing files. It's also about understanding what happened.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's where Timeline view comes in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In VS Code, you can open Timeline from the Explorer sidebar when a file is selected, or use the Command Palette to access history-related views. Timeline shows events associated with the selected file, including commits. You can browse backward through changes, open a particular commit entry, and inspect what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's blame, which has a dramatic name but a practical purpose.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very practical. Git blame shows who last changed each line and in which commit. In some setups it appears inline or in an extension-provided view. For screen reader users, the most important thing is whether the information is exposed accessibly. Sometimes the terminal or a text-based history command is easier to read than a dense visual annotation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also includes CLI history commands learners should recognize.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can view commit history for the whole repository with git log. For a specific file, git log double-dash path-to-file. To see what changed in each commit, use patch output. For line-level history, blame commands show authorship. You can show a specific commit's changes with git show commit-sha. You can compare two commits, filter commits by author, limit to recent days, inspect recent commits on the web, and review PR history on GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's an accessibility point here too: structured diff review often beats visually scanning for changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A diff viewer that lets you move hunk by hunk is often more efficient than searching manually through a changed file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which leads naturally to merge conflicts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Conflicts happen when Git can't automatically reconcile competing changes. VS Code displays conflict regions in the editor and often provides actions such as accepting the current change, the incoming change, both changes, or manually editing the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does a good screen reader workflow look like for resolving conflicts?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, identify which file has the conflict. Open it and navigate carefully through the conflict regions. If your setup exposes structured conflict actions accessibly, use them. If not, reading the conflict markers and editing manually may be necessary. Review the surrounding context so you understand what each version is trying to do.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter emphasizes accessible diff tools here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, because accessible diff navigation gives you structured, hunk-by-hunk movement instead of forcing you to hunt for markers in a long file. After choosing or combining content, save the file, stage it, and complete the merge commit. If the merge is not worth continuing, you can abort the merge and return to the pre-merge state.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So an aborted merge is not failure. It's a controlled retreat.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Sometimes stepping back is the best troubleshooting move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Stash management is another skill that helps when your work is in-between states.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should someone use stash?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use stash when you have uncommitted changes but need to switch tasks, change branches, pull updates safely, or test something without committing half-finished work. Think of it as temporarily setting your changes aside.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create a stash?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, the Command Palette may offer stash commands. In the terminal, you can stash all uncommitted changes with a message, which is especially helpful because named stashes are easier to identify later. If you also want to include untracked new files, use the include-untracked option.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you see what stashes exist?

speaker: ALEX
text: Either through the Command Palette if your interface exposes them, or in terminal with git stash list. To inspect a specific stash, use a show command that reveals its contents.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And restoring?

speaker: ALEX
text: Applying a stash restores the changes but keeps the stash entry. Popping a stash restores the changes and removes that stash entry in one step. You can apply the most recent stash or a specific stash by reference. That difference between apply and pop is important: apply is safer when you're unsure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And cleanup?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can drop a specific stash or clear all stashes, but clearing all should be done very carefully.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's the emergency recovery section, which I think comforts a lot of learners.

speaker: ALEX
text: It should. Git reflog is one of the best safety nets in Git. It records where your HEAD and branch references have been locally, even when commits seem lost after resets, rebases, or branch deletions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Local is the key word there.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Reflog is local-only. It's your machine's memory of reference movements. It is not a shared remote history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if you think you lost a commit, what's the recovery pattern?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, open the VS Code terminal and read the reflog to find the last good commit SHA. Second, preview that commit with a show command so you know it's the right one. Third, take the safer recovery route whenever possible: create a new branch at that commit. That preserves the current state while giving you access to the lost work. The riskier option is resetting your current branch back to that point.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you deleted a branch entirely?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use reflog to find the commit that branch pointed to, then recreate the branch at that SHA. This is one reason experienced Git users seem calm in situations that feel catastrophic to beginners.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because they know the history is often still recoverable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: The chapter also acknowledges that VS Code is not the only interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Right. There are alternatives depending on accessibility preferences and task type.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. GitHub Desktop can be appealing for a more guided visual workflow. GitHub CLI, the gh tool, is excellent for repository and pull request tasks and often works very well with screen readers when you like structured terminal output. And the core Git CLI remains the most universal interface, with common commands for status, add, commit, branch, switch, push, pull, fetch, stash, log, show, diff, and reflog.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the real goal is not loyalty to one interface. It's fluency across concepts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Beautifully said. Tools differ. The model stays the same.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, can we give learners a quick practical recap they can actually use the next time they sit down to work?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Start by checking your state: git status. Confirm your branch: git branch --show-current. If you need the repo locally, clone it and verify it opened correctly in Explorer. Create or switch to the correct branch before editing. Make one meaningful change. Stage intentionally, either whole file or selected chunks. Write a clear commit message. Commit locally. Push to GitHub, publishing the branch if needed. Open a pull request and include issue linkage like Closes number. If you need updates first, fetch or pull. If something feels risky, stash instead of discarding. If something seems lost, reflog may save you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for accessibility specifically?

speaker: ALEX
text: Prefer predictable keyboard-first workflows. The Command Palette is often the most screen-reader-friendly route in VS Code. Use the Source Control panel deliberately: know where the message field is, know where the file groups are, and use context menus when inline buttons are awkward. Verify feedback through notifications, status bar announcements, diffs, and terminal commands. Never rely on visual assumptions alone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: One more thing: the chapter includes a try-it prompt to practice clone, branch, and commit. That's worth doing even if you think you understood the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Git becomes clear through repetition. Practice the sequence until it feels boring. That's when it becomes dependable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone gets stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pause, check status, check branch, read the exact message Git or VS Code is giving you, and ask for help with those details. "It didn't work" is hard to debug. "Git says non-fast-forward on branch learn slash jamie after push" is very debug-friendly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is such a good learning habit.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And one final note: this chapter points learners toward authoritative sources too, especially the VS Code source control docs, GitHub's Git documentation, and the GitHub Accessibility Lab CLI guide. Those are excellent references as you keep practicing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the big takeaway is that accessible Git is absolutely possible, and a careful workflow beats a fast, fuzzy one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Clear state, explicit branches, deliberate staging, meaningful commits, and accessible review habits. That's a professional workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Thanks for learning with us.

speaker: JAMIE
text: We'll see you next time.
